aliases: mixed vegetable curry with coconut and yogurt

english: mixed vegetable curry with coconut and yogurt

food_subgroup: Mixed vegetable semi-dry curry with coconut and yogurt

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Aviyal. Nearest parent is a mixed vegetable curry. A new concept covering Kerala Sadya dishes is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed vegetable curry with coconut paste, South Indian style (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Aviyal; A03SM used as closest defensible parent for mixed vegetable curry preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Aviyal or Kerala mixed vegetable curry.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish serving of Aviyal at a Kerala or South Indian restaurant is approximately 150 g, typically served alongside rice as part of a Sadya or banana-leaf meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN; component data for Kerala Sadya vegetables, coconut, and yogurt
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Aviyal is not listed as a single composite entry in ICMR-NIN. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for the constituent vegetables (raw banana, drumstick, yam, carrot, beans, raw mango), fresh coconut, plain yogurt, and coconut oil, weighted by typical recipe proportions for Kerala Sadya Aviyal. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Vegetable Preparations with Coconut — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetable curry with high dietary fibre and fat content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Aviyal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: mixed vegetable preparations with coconut and high dietary fibre (GI range 45–52). The high fibre content (3.5 g/100 g), fat from coconut (7.5 g/100 g), and the presence of raw banana and yam (moderate-GI starchy vegetables) support a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (48 × 12.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 9.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 215 mg/100 g (323 mg per 150 g serving); partially offset by high potassium 285 mg/100 g (428 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Aviyal provides a moderate sodium load (~323 mg per serving); individuals managing hypertension should monitor portion size and limit added salt during preparation, while benefiting from the high potassium content from the mixed vegetables which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~48) and low glycemic load (~9 per 150 g serving); dietary fibre 3.5 g/100 g and coconut fat slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aviyal is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (~48), low glycemic load per serving (~9), and high dietary fibre content (5.3 g per 150 g serving) which collectively attenuate postprandial glucose response; coconut fat further slows gastric emptying.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.8 g/100 g (8.7 g per serving) primarily from coconut; trans fat is zero; cholesterol is negligible at 2 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aviyal contains a moderate saturated fat load (~8.7 g per 150 g serving) from coconut oil and fresh coconut, which warrants portion awareness for individuals with cardiovascular disease; however, the absence of trans fat, negligible cholesterol, and the antioxidant-rich vegetable content (vitamin C, vitamin A, folate) provide partially offsetting cardiovascular benefits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from drumstick (moringa), curcumin from turmeric, and diverse antioxidant-rich vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Aviyal may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of drumstick (moringa pods), the antioxidant content from the diverse vegetable medley, and the presence of iron (1.5 mg/100 g) and calcium (32 mg/100 g) which support musculoskeletal health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aviyal (also spelled Avial; Tamil: அவியல்) is a traditional Kerala Sadya dish and one of the most iconic preparations of South Indian cuisine. It is a semi-dry, richly fragrant curry made from a colourful medley of vegetables — typically raw banana (plantain), drumstick (moringa pods), yam, carrot, green beans, and raw mango — coated in a freshly ground paste of grated coconut, green chilli, and cumin. The vegetables are cut into uniform batons and simmered until just tender, then finished with a generous addition of whisked plain yogurt that thickens the sauce and imparts a characteristic mild sourness. The dish is completed with a drizzle of raw coconut oil and fresh curry leaves, which provide its distinctive aroma. Aviyal is nutritionally dense: it provides 118 kcal per 100 g, 2.2 g protein, 7.5 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut at 5.8 g/100 g), and 12.5 g carbohydrate with 3.5 g dietary fibre. It is a good source of potassium (285 mg/100 g), vitamin A (58 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin C (18 mg/100 g), and iron (1.5 mg/100 g). Its low glycemic index (~48) and low glycemic load per serving (~9) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium is moderate at 215 mg/100 g. Aviyal is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. A traditional Onam Sadya banana-leaf meal is considered incomplete without aviyal. In Singapore, it is served at Kerala and South Indian restaurants.
common_user_queries: is aviyal good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Aviyal is not listed as a single composite entry in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for the constituent vegetables, coconut, and yogurt, weighted by typical recipe proportions. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Aviyal as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN component data. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Aviyal is a vegetarian dish with no animal flesh; the trace amount from yogurt is below detection threshold and reported as zero by ICMR-NIN dairy component data. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no vitamin D-contributing ingredients are present; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional Aviyal preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods component data and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on mixed vegetable preparations with coconut and high dietary fibre.
